issues.description: The method's stated purpose is to avoid stack overflow on deeply nested expressions by using an explicit stack, but `binary_expression` pushes children onto the stack then immediately calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` which recurses into `lower_expr`. Similarly, `unary_expression` calls `self.lower_unary_expr(node)`, `function_call` calls `self.lower_function_call(node)`, and the `_` fallback calls `self.lower_expr(node)` — all fully recursive. The iterative wrapper provides no protection against stack overflow for any non-trivial expression tree.
issues.suggested_fix: A correct iterative lowering requires a two-phase approach: (1) a downward pass that pushes children with continuation markers onto the stack, and (2) an upward pass that pops lowered children to construct parent nodes. This is typically done with an enum like `enum Work { Lower(Node), BuildBinOp { op, span }, BuildUnOp { op, span }, ... }` and a separate operand stack. Alternatively, consider using `stacker::maybe_grow()` to extend the native stack on demand, which is far simpler and already used in similar Rust tree-walking scenarios.

issues.description: For `binary_expression`, children are pushed onto the stack AND `lower_binary_expr(node)` is called, pushing its result into `results`. When those children are later popped and processed, their results are also pushed into `results` as orphaned values not consumed by any parent. The final `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` would return the last-processed child's result (due to LIFO stack ordering), not the root expression. For example, `1 + 2` would return the lowered form of `2` (or `1`), not the `BinOp` expression.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the child-pushing in the binary_expression arm (and similarly for parenthesized_expression), or redesign the algorithm to properly track the relationship between parent results and child results using continuation frames.

issues.description: `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` returns the last element pushed to `results`, which due to LIFO stack processing is the result from the first-pushed (deepest) child, not the root node. The root is processed first (popped first from the initial push), so its result is `results[0]`. Subsequent children pushed during root processing produce `results[1..n]`. The method should return `results[0]` (or `results.first()`), not `results.last()`.
issues.suggested_fix: At minimum, change to `results.into_iter().next().flatten()` to return the root's result. But this still won't fix the fundamental design issues with orphaned child results.

issues.description: The method `lower_expr_iterative` is never called from anywhere in the codebase. All ~20 call sites continue to use `lower_expr`. Dead code adds maintenance burden and will drift out of sync with the real `lower_expr` dispatcher (which handles 19 expression kinds vs. 7 here).
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire this method into the call graph (replacing `lower_expr` at appropriate entry points) or remove it until the iterative approach is fully implemented and tested.

issues.description: No tests cover `lower_expr_iterative`. Given the complexity of iterative tree lowering and the multiple correctness issues identified, this method needs thorough testing — at minimum: simple literals, nested binary expressions (depth > 1), parenthesized expressions, mixed expression trees, and a deep-nesting stress test to validate the stack-overflow protection claim.
issues.suggested_fix: Add unit tests that compare `lower_expr_iterative` output against `lower_expr` output for a variety of expression trees, plus a deep-nesting test (e.g., 10,000-deep parenthesized chain) that would overflow with recursion but succeed with the iterative version.

issues.description: The condition `child.is_named() && child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` has redundant parts. In tree-sitter, `(` and `)` are anonymous (unnamed) nodes, so `child.is_named()` already excludes them. The extra `kind()` checks are dead conditions.
issues.suggested_fix: Simplify to `if child.is_named()` to match the pattern used in the `binary_expression` arm at line 1718.

issues.description: The existing `lower_expr` handles 19 expression kinds (binary, unary, function_call, conditional, lambda, quantifier, match, member_access, qualified_access, instance_qualified_access, index_access, list_literal, set_literal, map_literal, range, ad_hoc_selector, quantity_literal, auto, and all literals). This iterative version only explicitly handles 7 kinds and falls back to recursive `lower_expr` for the remaining 12, further undermining the stack-overflow prevention goal.
issues.suggested_fix: If implementing iterative lowering, all recursive expression kinds need iterative handling — especially `conditional` (3 recursive children), `match` (n recursive arms), and `member_access`/`qualified_access` chains which are the most likely to nest deeply in practice.

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The `lower_expr_iterative` method is fundamentally broken in three ways: (1) it falls back to recursive calls for all non-trivial expression types, defeating its stated purpose of preventing stack overflow; (2) the stack/results bookkeeping is incorrect — for binary expressions, children are pushed for later processing AND the parent is recursively lowered, producing orphaned results; (3) `results.last()` returns the deepest child's result rather than the root expression due to LIFO processing order. The method is also dead code (never called), has no tests, only covers 7 of 19 expression kinds, and contains redundant tree-sitter node checks. The correct approach for iterative tree lowering in Rust requires either a two-phase work-stack with continuation frames, or a simpler solution like `stacker::maybe_grow()` for on-demand stack extension.
